> **Goal:** build a two-class classifier **from scratch** — the sigmoid model,
> the log-loss cost, its gradient, and a NumPy gradient-descent training loop —
> and understand *why* each piece is what it is.

## Why a mathematician should care

Classification is "which bin does this belong to?" rather than "what number is
it?". Logistic regression is the simplest, most-used answer for two classes,
and it is a beautiful little optimization problem: a convex cost whose gradient
has the same clean form as linear regression. Master it and the rest of
supervised learning (softmax, neural nets) is the same recipe scaled up.

## Classification vs. regression

| | Regression | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| Output $y$ | a real number | a label, e.g. $\{0,1\}$ |
| Model gives | the value | a probability $p = P(y=1\mid x)$ |
| Cost | mean squared error | cross-entropy (log-loss) |

## Core ideas in this chapter

### The sigmoid
$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$
maps any real $z$ into $(0,1)$, so it can serve as a probability. It is
S-shaped, with $\sigma(0)=\tfrac12$, and has the tidy derivative
$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\,(1-\sigma(z))$.

### The model
$$p = \sigma(w\cdot x + b) = P(y=1\mid x).$$
With a **bias column** of 1's glued onto $X$, this becomes $p = \sigma(X\theta)$
with $\theta = (b, w_1, \dots, w_d)$.

### The decision boundary
Predict class 1 when $p \ge 0.5$, i.e. when $w\cdot x + b \ge 0$. The set
$w\cdot x + b = 0$ is a straight line — logistic regression is a **linear**
classifier.

### The cost: log-loss (not MSE)
MSE-through-a-sigmoid is non-convex, so we use the convex **cross-entropy**:
$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\Big[y_i\log p_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-p_i)\Big],
\qquad p_i = \sigma(X_i\theta).$$
Use a clip/epsilon on $p$ so `log` never sees an exact 0 or 1.

### The gradient (clean result)
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial\theta} = \frac{1}{n}\,X^{\top}\big(\sigma(X\theta) - y\big),$$
and gradient descent updates $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \alpha\,\partial J/\partial\theta$.

### Accuracy
The fraction of labels predicted correctly:
$\text{accuracy} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \mathbb{1}[\hat y_i = y_i]$.

### Multi-class: softmax
For $K>2$ classes, replace the sigmoid with
$\text{softmax}(z)_k = e^{z_k}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ and use multi-class
cross-entropy. Same training recipe, a matrix of weights instead of a vector.
Notebook **11b** builds this in full — see below.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| "Regression" in the name | it is a **classification** method |
| Why not MSE | MSE + sigmoid is non-convex; log-loss is convex |
| `log(0)` | is $-\infty$; clip probabilities into $(0,1)$ first |
| Decision boundary | a straight line; data must be roughly linearly separable |
| Same gradient as linear regression | $\frac1n X^\top(p-y)$ — only $p$ changed |

## The notebook

- `11_logistic_regression.ipynb` — the full two-class build: sigmoid plot, model,
  log-loss with a "why" plot, the gradient, a two-blob synthetic dataset,
  from-scratch gradient descent, loss curve, learned boundary, accuracy, and a
  softmax peek. Includes 3 worked exercises and 4 homework problems.
- `11b_softmax_multiclass.ipynb` — the full multi-class build: softmax (with a
  numerically-stable version and *why* it works), the model $Z = XW + b$,
  one-hot labels, categorical cross-entropy, the clean gradient
  $\mathrm{d}Z=(P-Y)/N$ verified with a numerical gradient check, a three-blob
  synthetic dataset, from-scratch gradient descent, loss curve, multi-class
  decision regions, accuracy, and a check that $K=2$ reduces to logistic
  regression. Includes 3 worked exercises and 4 homework problems.

## After this chapter

You'll be ready to view a small **neural network** as stacked logistic-style
units trained by the same gradient-descent machinery.

## 📚 Further reading

- Andrew Ng — [Machine Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction) (Coursera, DeepLearning.AI) — logistic regression and the sigmoid.
- Stanford [CS229 lecture notes](https://cs229.stanford.edu/) — log-loss and the gradient derived fully.
- [Deep Learning book](https://www.deeplearningbook.org) — Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (free) — softmax and cross-entropy.
